daily_brief: The US-Iran conflict dominated international headlines as American forces conducted their 11th consecutive night of strikes against Iranian targets, with Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth revealing the war has cost $37.5 billion to date [BBC, Al Jazeera, Globe and Mail, The Hindu]. President Trump threatened to strike Iran's underground nuclear facility near Natanz, while Secretary of State Rubio stated the US remains open to negotiations but accused Tehran of not being serious [Al Jazeera, BBC]. In Ukraine, President Zelensky dismissed army commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi following days of street protests triggered by the earlier removal of popular Defense Minister Mykhailo Fedorov, replacing him with General Mykhailo Drapatyi [BBC, Le Monde, RFI]. France became the first European country to pass legislation banning social media for children under 15, with President Macron hailing it as a major step forward in child protection [BBC, RFI]. US-Canada relations deteriorated further as Ottawa cancelled a joint ceremony for the Gordie Howe International Bridge after Trump announced 50% tariffs on most Canadian goods, invoking the 1930 Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act [Globe and Mail, The Hindu]. Meanwhile, Germany and Azerbaijan signed strategic economic agreements as Berlin seeks to diversify energy supplies away from Russia [RFI].

top_stories.headline: US Launches 11th Consecutive Night of Strikes on Iran as War Costs Reach $37.5 Billion
bullets: US Central Command conducted strikes targeting Iranian military operations centres, maritime capabilities, aircraft hangars, drone storage facilities, and military logistics infrastructure
Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth testified the war has cost $37.5 billion, up from $29 billion in mid-May, and requested $67 billion in supplemental Pentagon funding

Air defences were activated in Tehran, with explosions reported in Tabriz, Chabahar, Konarak, and Bushehr; Iran warned of reprisals after Trump threatened to strike the underground nuclear site near Natanz
Secretary of State Rubio stated the US is open to negotiations but accused Iran of not being serious about talks, warning that Iranian control of the Strait of Hormuz would set a dangerous precedent

sources.name: BBC World
sources.framing_note: Focused on military operations and Trump's threat to nuclear facilities

sources.name: Al Jazeera
sources.framing_note: Emphasized Rubio's comments on negotiations and included Iranian state media reports on air defence activation

sources.name: The Hindu
sources.framing_note: Detailed Hegseth's Senate testimony and Democratic criticism of war spending

sources.name: Globe and Mail
sources.framing_note: Highlighted congressional pushback and concerns about 'another forever war'

sources.name: The Economist
sources.framing_note: Analysis piece comparing situation to Obama's foreign policy approach

top_stories.headline: Zelensky Dismisses Army Chief Syrskyi After Days of Street Protests Over Military Leadership
bullets: Ukrainian President Zelensky fired Commander-in-Chief Oleksandr Syrskyi following the earlier dismissal of Defense Minister Mykhailo Fedorov, which triggered nationwide protests
Syrskyi, 60, was replaced by General Mykhailo Drapatyi, 43, a popular battle-hardened commander who thanked his predecessor for strengthening the Ukrainian army
Fedorov, 35, had been praised for modernizing the armed forces and fighting corruption, while Syrskyi was seen as a Soviet-school commander resistant to radical changes

Protests entered their fifth day with demonstrators in Kyiv demanding Syrskyi's removal and Fedorov's return, representing Zelensky's most urgent domestic political challenge since the full-scale invasion

sources.name: BBC World
sources.framing_note: Balanced coverage of both commanders' records and Drapatyi's appointment

sources.name: Le Monde
sources.framing_note: Article headline available but content not accessible

sources.name: RFI English
sources.framing_note: Emphasized the political crisis and pressure on Zelensky, included protester testimonies about personal losses

top_stories.headline: France Becomes First European Country to Ban Social Media for Under-15s
bullets: French Parliament approved legislation requiring age verification for all social media users, banning those under 15 from accessing platforms from January 2027
The law will be implemented in two stages: new accounts blocked for under-15s from September 2026, and verification required for all existing accounts by January 2027
President Macron hailed the move as a major step forward, stating France is leading Europe in protecting children and teenagers
Critics raised concerns over privacy, the efficacy of age-verification tools, and the risk of young people bypassing restrictions

sources.name: BBC World
sources.framing_note: Detailed implementation timeline and noted skepticism about viability

sources.name: RFI English
sources.framing_note: Emphasized France as first EU country to follow Australia's lead, included parliamentary vote count of 279-81

top_stories.headline: Canada Cancels Joint Bridge Ceremony with US After Trump Announces 50% Tariffs
bullets: Canada cancelled the planned joint US-Canada ceremony for the Gordie Howe International Bridge connecting Detroit and Windsor after Trump announced 50% tariffs on most Canadian goods
Trump invoked Section 338 of the 1930 Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act, accusing Canada of discriminating against US automobiles, alcohol, and dairy products
Canada will hold its own ceremony on July 24, with the bridge still expected to open to traffic on July 27
The tariff list targets hundreds of Canadian goods including extensive alcohol-related products, prompting observers to note its unusual focus on cocktail ingredients

sources.name: The Hindu
sources.framing_note: Straightforward reporting on ceremony cancellation and infrastructure minister's statement

sources.name: Globe and Mail
sources.framing_note: Extensive coverage including historical context of Smoot-Hawley Act and detailed analysis of tariff list contents

top_stories.headline: Germany and Azerbaijan Sign Strategic Economic Agreements to Deepen Energy and Trade Ties
bullets: German Chancellor Friedrich Merz and Azerbaijani President Ilham Aliyev signed a Joint Declaration on Strategic Agenda and established an Azerbaijan-Germany Business Council
Azerbaijan began supplying gas to Germany this year as the EU moves away from Russian energy imports
Merz described the South Caucasus as a key connectivity hub linking Europe and Asia, referencing the Middle Corridor trade route that bypasses Russia
The talks also addressed Azerbaijan's reconciliation process with Armenia, with Merz congratulating Aliyev on progress toward a peace agreement

sources.name: RFI English
sources.framing_note: Comprehensive coverage of economic agreements and regional geopolitical context including Armenia relations

additional_stories.headline: Ukrainian drones strike Wildberries warehouses in Russia for second time in four days, injuring 15 workers
Health worker evacuated from DRC being monitored for Ebola at London hospital as precautionary measure
Indian opposition leader Rahul Gandhi released after detention during student-led protests over education system
Trump administration approves nuclear deal potentially allowing Saudi Arabia uranium enrichment capability
Protests erupt in Bologna after Moroccan-born man dies while being restrained by police, injuring 64 officers
New Zealand issues Level 3 travel advisory urging citizens to avoid Middle East due to US-Iran conflict
France and Germany developing joint roadmap to address EU's growing trade deficit with China by September
At least 53 people dead in ferry disaster in Guyana
Indonesia's free meal program chief resigns citing health after budget cuts and corruption scandals
UN report finds one in three people globally cannot afford a healthy diet despite decline in hunger rates
